sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: UP.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 123, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 122 actions; a strong score needs about 55 or fewer. You are 2.2x over the target.
NET-ZERO WASTE: your last 102 actions returned the board to a state already seen 102 actions ago — that round-trip made no progress yet still cost 102 actions. Do not extend-and-retract or oscillate; a sequence that ends where it began is pure waste.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Looking at the history, I can see that the initial frame had:
- id=11: g, 30 pixels, boundary=[[24, 25], [24, 38]] (bordered block at top)
- id=12: p, 84 pixels (purple content)
- id=13: W, 100 pixels (white block, 100 pixels = merged state)
- id=14: c, 64 pixels (bottom bar)
So the initial state had a merged white block (100 pixels) below the bordered block. There was no separate purple and white block - just one merged white block.
But the current state has:
- id=13: p, 50 pixels (purple block)
- id=14: W, 50 pixels (white block)
So somehow the merged white block was split into purple and white blocks. This happened at some point during my actions.
Looking at the history frames 63-72, I can see that the bordered block was at a different position (rows 32-40 or 45-52). It seems like the bordered block moved around a lot.
The key insight is that the initial state had a merged white block (100 pixels), and the current state has two separate blocks (purple 50 + white 50). So the merged block was split at some point.
Now, the question is: what's the goal? Looking at the reference panel:
- White on top
- Purple on bottom
And the current state has:
- Purple on top
- White on bottom
These are opposite. So I need to swap them.
But I've been unable to move the bordered block up or down. The bordered block is stuck at rows 24-32.
Wait, let me look at the initial state more carefully. The initial state had:
- Bordered block at rows 24-32 (same as current)
- Merged white block at rows 34-43 (100 pixels = 10x10)
So the initial state had the bordered block at the top and a merged white block below it. The current state has the bordered block at the top and two separate blocks (purple + white) below it.
The difference is that the merged white block was split into purple and white blocks. This must have happened when I pressed UP or DOWN at some point.
Actually, looking at the initial state, the merged block was white (100 pixels). But the current state has purple (50) and white (50). So the merged block was split, and the top part became purple.
Wait, but the initial state had a merged white block. How did it become purple and white?
Let me think about this differently. Maybe the initial state is the target state, and I need to restore it. The initial state had:
- Bordered block with purple content at top
- Merged white block below
But the current state has:
- Bordered block with purple content at top
- Purple block below
- White block below
So I need to merge the purple and white blocks back into a single white block. But how?
Actually, wait. Let me re-examine the initial state. The initial frame had:
- id=13: W, 100 pixels (merged white block)
But the reference panel shows white on top and purple on bottom. If the merged block is white, and the bordered content is purple, then:
- Bordered content: purple (bottom of reference)
- Merged block: white (top of reference)
This matches the reference panel if the reference is read as:
- Top: merged block color (white)
- Bottom: bordered content color (purple)
So the initial state matches the reference panel! The goal is to restore the initial state.
But the current state has the merged block split into purple and white. I need to merge them back into a single white block.
To merge the blocks, I need to:
1. Move the bordered block to the bottom (below the colored blocks)
2. Press SPACE to merge the blocks
But the bordered block is stuck at the top. I can't move it down.
Wait, let me look at the history more carefully. In frame 67, the bordered block was at rows 40-44 (id=13: g, 43 pixels, boundary=[[40, 20], [41, 20]]). So the bordered block can be at the bottom.
But in the current state, the bordered block is at rows 24-32, and pressing DOWN doesn't move it. This suggests that the bordered block can only move when it's adjacent to the colored blocks.
In the current state, there's a gap (row 33) between the bordered block (rows 24-32) and the purple block (rows 34-38). Maybe the bordered block can't move because of this gap.
But in the initial state, the merged white block was at rows 34-43, and the bordered block was at rows 24-32. There was also a gap (row 33) between them. So the gap doesn't prevent the bordered block from being at the top.
Hmm, let me think about this differently. Maybe the bordered block can only move when the colored blocks are in a certain configuration. For example, maybe the bordered block can only move when there are two separate blocks, not when they're merged.
In the initial state, the blocks were merged (100 pixels). The bordered block was at the top. I pressed some actions that split the merged block into two separate blocks (purple 50 + white 50). Now the bordered block is stuck at the top.
Wait, but in the history, I was able to move the bordered block up and down when the blocks were separate. So the bordered block can move when the blocks are separate.
Let me look at the history more carefully. In frame 63, the bordered block was at rows 32-40 (id=11: g, 30 pixels, boundary=[[32, 17], [32, 25]]). The colored blocks were at rows 34-38 (purple, 65 pixels) and rows 39-43 (white, 35 pixels). So the bordered block was overlapping with the colored blocks.
Actually, I think the bordered block's position changed because the game layout changed. The bordered block was at a different column (17-25) in frame 63, compared to the current position (25-38). So the bordered block moved horizontally as well as vertically.
This suggests that the game has multiple positions for the bordered block, and it can move to different positions.
Let me try pressing LEFT or RIGHT to see if the bordered block moves horizontally.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Let me try LEFT and RIGHT to see if the bordered block moves horizontally
action(['LEFT'])

seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("After LEFT:")
for node in seg['nodes']:
    if node['id'] >= 10:
        print(f"  id={node['id']} color={node['color']} pixels={node['pixels']} boundary={node['boundary'][:2]}")
print("Board changed:", last_action_result.get('board_changed'))
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
